validation.comparison_result: Q6 match: true (1 row expected, 1 row actual)

Q6.notes: Result matches ground truth. Current time: 10ms. Target: 19.28ms (DuckDB min). GenDB already faster than DuckDB!

Q6.bottleneck: Already achieving excellent performance (10ms vs DuckDB 19ms). Further optimization potential exists in I/O efficiency and SIMD utilization.
Q6.root_cause: Current implementation uses 64 threads with AVX2 SIMD and zone map pruning. Performance is already better than DuckDB. However, the SIMD implementation has inefficiencies: (1) SIMD evaluation produces mask but then falls back to scalar processing for matching lanes, negating SIMD benefits; (2) madvise uses MADV_SEQUENTIAL for random access pattern (zone map pruning skips blocks); (3) no prefetching hints to pipeline I/O with computation; (4) SIMD code uses unaligned loads which are slower than aligned loads.

analysis.overall: Q6 is currently faster than DuckDB (10ms vs 19ms), which is excellent. The query correctly implements parallel scanning with zone map pruning and SIMD predicate evaluation. However, there are several micro-optimizations that could push performance even further: (1) improve SIMD to do full vectorized aggregation instead of scalar fallback, (2) optimize madvise hints for sparse access pattern, (3) reduce thread count or morsel size to minimize scheduling overhead for such a small result set, (4) investigate data alignment and prefetching strategies.

performance_optimizations.description: Current SIMD implementation (lines 169-211 in q6.cpp) evaluates predicates with SIMD but then falls back to scalar processing for matching lanes. This defeats the purpose of SIMD. Improvement: (1) Use SIMD-based masked load/multiply to compute `extendedprice * discount` for qualifying rows directly in SIMD registers, (2) Accumulate partial sums in __m256i registers and only reduce to scalar at block end, (3) This eliminates the scalar loop inside the SIMD section and enables full vectorization. Reference: knowledge/parallelism/simd.md describes 'Horizontal aggregation' and 'Branchless SIMD loops'. This should improve throughput by 1.5-2x on filtered computation.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.5-2x speedup (10ms -> 5-7ms) by eliminating scalar fallback and fully vectorizing the aggregation path

performance_optimizations.technique: Optimize madvise hints for sparse access pattern
performance_optimizations.description: Current code (line 87 in q6.cpp) uses MADV_SEQUENTIAL for all columns. However, zone map pruning means we're skipping blocks, creating a sparse access pattern. Change: (1) Use MADV_RANDOM or no madvise for columns when zone maps are available (since access pattern is unpredictable), (2) Alternatively, use MADV_WILLNEED to prefetch only blocks that pass zone map filtering, (3) For HDD storage (detected: rota=1), prefetching becomes even more critical to pipeline I/O. Reference: knowledge/storage/persistent-storage.md line 9 mentions 'MADV_RANDOM for index lookups'. Expected improvement: 10-20% reduction in I/O wait time, especially on HDD.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-20% speedup (10ms -> 8-9ms) by better aligning I/O hints with actual access pattern

performance_optimizations.technique: Optimize thread count for small workload
performance_optimizations.description: Current code spawns 64 threads (line 289-290 in q6.cpp) for a highly selective query (2.4% selectivity = ~1.44M qualifying rows). With 100K row blocks and zone map pruning, many blocks are skipped. Thread spawning and synchronization overhead may dominate for such small work. Experiment: (1) Test with 8-16 threads instead of 64 to reduce contention on atomic next_block counter and thread management overhead, (2) Increase morsel/block size to 200K-500K rows to give each thread more work per scheduling unit. Reference: knowledge/parallelism/thread-parallelism.md mentions 'Morsel size tuning' for cache locality. Expected: 5-15% improvement from reduced thread overhead.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-15% speedup (10ms -> 8.5-9.5ms) by reducing thread management overhead

performance_optimizations.technique: Add data prefetching in worker threads
performance_optimizations.description: Current worker threads (lines 229-258) fetch next block atomically, check zone map, then process. For HDD (detected), disk latency dominates. Improvement: (1) Prefetch next N blocks ahead while processing current block using __builtin_prefetch or separate prefetch thread, (2) This pipelines I/O with computation, hiding disk latency. Reference: knowledge/storage/persistent-storage.md mentions 'MADV_WILLNEED for prefetching'. Expected: 10-20% improvement on HDD by overlapping I/O.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-20% speedup (10ms -> 8-9ms) on HDD by pipelining I/O with computation

performance_optimizations.description: Current SIMD code (lines 180-182) uses _mm256_loadu_si256 (unaligned load) which is ~50% slower than aligned loads. Improvement: (1) Ensure column data arrays are 32-byte aligned (use posix_memalign or alignas), (2) Switch to _mm256_load_si256 for aligned loads, (3) Handle misaligned start/end with scalar code. Reference: knowledge/parallelism/simd.md line 21 mentions 'Aligned vs unaligned loads: prefer aligned loads for ~2x better throughput'. Expected: 5-10% improvement in SIMD filter throughput.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% speedup (10ms -> 9-9.5ms) from faster memory loads in SIMD path

performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation evaluates predicates in order: shipdate, discount, quantity (lines 202-206). Based on workload analysis: shipdate selectivity = 1 year / 6.3 years = ~16%, discount = 3/11 = ~27%, quantity < 24 / 50 = ~48%. Optimal order should be: shipdate (most selective), discount, then quantity. Current code already follows this order, but scalar evaluation still checks all predicates. Improvement: (1) Add early exit in scalar path after shipdate fails (most common case), (2) This reduces branch mispredictions. Reference: knowledge/query-execution/scan-filter-optimization.md lines 9-12 describe 'Most selective predicates first' and 'Short-circuit compound predicates'. Expected: 5-10% improvement from better branch prediction.

summary: Q6 iteration 0 passes all tests with excellent performance (10ms, faster than DuckDB's 19ms). The implementation correctly uses parallel scanning (64 threads), zone map pruning, and AVX2 SIMD for predicate evaluation. However, there are several micro-optimizations available: (1) SIMD code currently falls back to scalar processing after predicate evaluation, losing vectorization benefits; (2) madvise hints don't match the sparse access pattern caused by zone map pruning; (3) thread count (64) may be excessive for this selective query, causing scheduling overhead; (4) unaligned SIMD loads are slower than aligned; (5) no prefetching for HDD I/O. Recommend prioritizing SIMD aggregation fix for biggest impact (1.5-2x), followed by I/O optimizations for HDD.
